question: Which group has the ultimate control over a corporation?

options.D: Directors
options.E: Chief executive officer

question: A classified board is one which has:

options.A: representation from various classes of stock.
options.B: terms that expire at different times.
options.D: directors elected solely by one class of shareholders.
options.E: directors that have been assigned differing numbers of votes per seat.

question: Preferred stock dividends:

options.A: become a debt of the firm if unpaid.
options.C: are only paid if common stock dividends are also paid.
options.D: have priority over debt interest payments but not common stock dividends.
options.E: are a tax-deductible business expense.

question: Firms may prefer to issue cumulative preferred stock rather than debt for which reason?

options.A: If there is no current taxable income, preferred stock dividends are automatically voided.
options.B: Preferred stock has no voting rights but debt does.
options.C: Preferred dividends provide a tax shield but debt does not.
options.D: Corporate investors can receive a tax break on dividends.
options.E: Dividend payments are tax deductible, interest on debt is not.

question: Which one of the following statements about preferred stock is true?

options.A: Unlike dividends paid on common stock, dividends paid on preferred stock are a tax- deductible expense.
options.B: Dividends on preferred stock payable during the next twelve months are considered to be a corporate liability.
options.C: If preferred dividends are non-cumulative, then preferred dividends not paid in a particular year will be carried forward to the next year.
options.D: There is no significant difference in the voting rights granted to preferred and common shareholders.
options.E: Preferred stock usually has a stated liquidating value of $100 per share.

question: A grant of authority allowing someone else to vote shares of stock that you own is called a:

options.D: restricted conveyance.
options.E: general right of execution.

question: Not paying dividends on a cumulative preferred issue may result in:

options.A: preferred dividend arrearages that can be eliminated only after all common dividends are paid.
options.B: increased taxes based on the amount of the dividend arrearage.
options.C: the permanent forfeiture of all unpaid past dividends but the resumption of future dividends.
options.D: the issuer being forced into repaying all preferred shareholders the stated value of their shares.
options.E: voting rights being granted to preferred shareholders.

question: ABC owns 15 percent of XYZ Corporation. What tax benefit does ABC derive from this situation?

options.A: ABC receives no tax benefit but XYZ is only taxed on 30 percent of its net income.
options.B: ABC benefits because it is able to treat any XYZ dividends it receives as interest income.
options.C: Fifty percent of the dividends paid by XYZ to ABC is exempt from income taxes.
options.D: ABC can exclude 30 percent of any XYZ dividends received from its taxable income.
options.E: All dividend income ABC receives from XYZ is tax-exempt.

question: There are three directors' seats up for election. If you own 1,000 shares of stock and have been granted a total of 3,000 votes to cast in a single election, then the firm uses the voting procedure referred to as:

options.D: straight voting.
options.E: market share voting.

question: There are three directors' seats up for election. If you own 1,000 shares of stock and you can cast 1,000 votes in each of the three elections, then the firm uses the voting procedure referred to as:

options.D: straight voting.
options.E: market share voting.

question: If a group other than current management solicits the authority to vote shares as part of their effort to replace the current management team, a ________ is said to occur.

options.D: vote of confidence
options.E: seniority turnover

question: When shareholders are granted preemptive rights, they obtain the right:

options.A: to elect members to the board of directors.
options.B: to share proportionally in regular and liquidating dividends.
options.C: of first refusal for their proportionate percentage of new shares offered.
options.D: to receive dividends prior to any preferred shareholders.
options.E: to resell their shares to the issuer at any time at a predetermined price.

question: Different classes of stock usually are issued to:

options.A: allow a certain group to maintain ownership control while reducing that group's equity position.
options.D: extract perquisites from one class of shareholders without the other class of shareholders knowing.
options.E: distinguish the time periods in which the various shares were issued.

question: Assuming control of a corporation would be hardest to obtain if the firm uses which type of voting?

options.A: Cumulative voting with annual elections for each seat
options.B: Cumulative voting with an annual board
options.C: Straight voting with a staggered board
options.D: Cumulative voting with a staggered board
options.E: Straight voting with annual elections for each seat

question: Which one of these is not a right generally granted to shareholders?

options.A: Right to elect individuals to the board of directors
options.B: Right to purchase shares of any new stock issue
options.D: Right to vote to approve or reject a merger offer
options.E: First right to liquidation proceeds

question: Which one of the following statements is true?

options.A: Bondholders are generally granted voting rights equal to those of common shareholders.
options.B: Payments of both interest and dividends are tax-deductible as business expenses.
options.C: Unpaid common stock dividends can force a firm into liquidation.
options.D: Debt increases the possibility of financial distress.
options.E: Debt holders have a residual claim on a firm's assets.

question: Unsecured corporate debt is commonly referred to as:

options.D: protected debt.
options.E: collateralized debt.

question: Bonds that grant the issuer the right to extinguish the debt prior to maturity are referred to as which type of bond?

options.D: Subordinated bond
options.E: Covenant bond

question: The written agreement between a corporation and its bondholders is called the:

options.D: conveyance.
options.E: legal understanding.

question: Ideally, corporations try to create securities that have the tax benefits:

options.A: of equity but the bankruptcy benefits of debt.
options.D: of debt and the equity benefits of dividends.
options.E: of debt but the bankruptcy benefits of equity.

question: If an issuer retires a debt issue before maturity, the specific amount paid to do so is called the:

options.D: the discount.
options.E: par or face amount.

question: If a debt is subordinated, it:

options.A: has a higher priority status than secured creditors.
options.C: must give preference to the secured creditors in the event of default.
options.D: has been issued because the company is in default.
options.E: is treated as an equity security.

question: The written agreement between a corporation and its bondholders might contain a prohibition against paying dividends in excess of current earnings. This prohibition is an example of a(n):

options.D: negative covenant.
options.E: put provision.

question: Which one of these is not included in the indenture?

options.D: Call provision
options.E: Repayment arrangements

question: If a bond issue is callable, the call price generally is:

options.B: variable based on the market rate of interest.
options.D: constant over the life of the debt.
options.E: set so it decreases as the bond approaches maturity.

question: Historically in the U.S., corporate bonds have generally been issued with a par value of:
A blanket mortgage is securitized by:

options.D: the good faith and credit of the borrower.
options.E: the borrower's inventories and accounts receivables.

question: Sinking fund arrangements are least apt to contain which one of these requirements?

options.A: A deferred provision for the first few years
options.B: A one-time repayment of the entire principal and interest at maturity
options.D: Equal payments of principal over the life of the bond
options.E: Sufficient payments over the bonds' life to retire the entire bond issue

question: If a bond has a make-whole call provision, the:

options.A: call premium can be either positive or negative.
options.B: bond's market price will always equal its face value.
options.C: bondholder will receive the face value amount plus interest if the bond is called.
options.D: bondholder will receive the face value amount minus any interest paid to date if the bond is called.
options.E: call price will increase as interest rates decrease.

question: Which one of these is a positive covenant?

options.A: The firm must maintain a current ratio of 1.2 or better.
options.B: The firm will not issue any debt with higher seniority.
options.C: The firm cannot be acquired in a friendly takeover.
options.D: No dividend increases will be allowed.
options.E: The market debt-equity ratio cannot exceed .60.

question: Which one of these applies to floating-rate bonds?

options.A: Bondholders can generally redeem their bonds at par at any time.
options.B: Coupon payments are variable while the par value is fixed.
options.C: Interest adjustments are accrued and paid on the maturity date.
options.D: Coupon payments are fixed but the par value is variable.
options.E: Bondholders frequently are granted a put provision at the current market price.

question: Which type of bond grants the bond holder the right to force the bond's issuer to repay the bond at a stated price given that a certain situation(s) occurs?

options.D: Income bond
options.E: Warrant bond

question: Bonds with attached warrants are frequently issued:

options.C: with an attached share of preferred stock.
options.D: with a share purchase price set equal to the market price at time of share purchase.
options.E: with an attached share of common stock.

question: Which type of bond only pays coupon payments if it can do so from the income earned by the firm?

options.D: LIBOR-based bond
options.E: Income bond

question: Which type of bond is a city or state most likely to use as a means of offsetting their cost of damages caused by a hurricane?

options.D: Put bond
options.E: CoCo bond

question: Which set of circumstances would best ensure the price of a bond with attached warrants will increase given no change in the bond's credit quality or terms?

options.A: An increase in both the market rate of interest and the underlying stock price
options.B: A decrease in the market rate of interest and an increase in the underlying stock price
options.C: An increase in the market rate of interest and a decrease in the underlying stock price
options.D: A decrease in both the market rate of interest and the underlying stock price
options.E: A decrease in the market rate of interest with no change in the underlying stock price

question: A revolving bank line of credit:

options.A: generally requires the borrower to borrow the entire credit line amount at some point in time.
options.B: generally involves a fee charged to the borrower on the unused portion of the revolver.
options.C: may only be offered for periods of one year or less.
options.D: is generally free of charge until money is actually borrowed.
options.E: allows the borrower to determine the amount of credit to be granted.

question: Which one of these statements correctly applies to either a leveraged or an unleveraged syndicated loan?

options.A: The loan will always be rated as investment grade.
options.B: The loan may not be publicly traded.
options.C: The loan arranger is not involved with the actual lending.
options.D: Each bank that participates negotiates the terms for its portion of the overall loan.
options.E: Each bank has its own loan agreement with the borrowers.

question: Which characteristic does not apply to Eurobonds?

options.C: Always denominated in a single currency
options.D: Generally denominated in the issuer's home currency
options.E: Issued in multiple countries

question: Bulldog bonds are associated with which country?

options.D: Germany
options.E: Morocco

question: Rembrandt bonds are associated with which country(ies)?

options.D: Belgium
options.E: Austria and Hungary

question: The term financial deficit is defined as the:

options.A: loss realized on bonds that are sold for less than their purchase price.
options.B: amount needed to fund all interest payments on currently outstanding debt.
options.C: total amount of cash flow required from all sources to meet the needs of the uses of cash.
options.D: amount of cash flow that must be funded internally.
options.E: uses of cash flow minus the cash flow available from internal sources.

question: What is the predominant source of financing for positive NPV projects by U.S. nonfinancial corporations?

options.D: Publicly-issued debt
options.E: Privately-issued debt

question: Recently, U.S. nonfinancial corporations have been:

options.B: issuing new shares of stock in record numbers.
options.D: paying off external debt at a record pace.
options.E: net issuers of stock.

question: Since 1975 U.S. nonfinancial corporations have tended to have debt-equity ratios that are:

options.A: steadily rising due to the low interest rate environment.
options.D: relatively stable over time.
options.E: relatively unaffected by stock market movements.

question: Financial economists prefer to use market values rather than book values when measuring debt ratios because market values are:

options.B: a better reflection of current information.
options.D: used by Standard & Poor's to measure credit worthiness.
options.E: most commonly required by bond covenants.

question: There are three seats on the board of directors of MMT, Inc., up for election. The firm has 175,000 shares of stock outstanding and uses cumulative voting. Each share is granted one vote per open seat. You currently own 10,000 shares that have a market value of $23 each. How much must you spend, if anything, to acquire sufficient shares to guarantee your election to the board? Assume no one else votes for you.
explanation: Total shares needed = [1/(3 + 1)](175,000) + 1 Total shares needed = 43,751 Cost to acquire shares = (43,751 − 10,000)($23) Cost to acquire shares = $776,273

question: There are seven seats on the board of directors of Furniture Unlimited up for election. The firm has 246,500 shares of stock outstanding and uses straight voting. Each share is granted one vote for each open seat. How many shares must you control if you want to guarantee your election to the board assuming no one else votes for you?

There are five seats on the board of directors of Atlas Corp. up for election. The firm has 120,000 shares of stock outstanding and uses cumulative voting. Each share is granted one vote per open seat. How many shares must you control if you want to guarantee your election to the board assuming no one else votes for you?

DLT has cumulative preferred stock outstanding that calls for quarterly dividend payments of $1.50 each. Due to its financial situation, the firm has not paid these preferred dividends for the past two quarters. What amount per share must be paid to the preferred shareholders this quarter if the firm also wants to pay a dividend on its common stock?
explanation: Preferred dividend requirement = 3($1.50) Preferred dividend requirement = $4.50

question: Analysts estimate that one year from today, a bond has a probability of 40 percent of being priced at $950 and a probability of 60 percent of being priced at $1,050. The bond is also callable at any time at $1,010. What is the expected value of this bond in one year?

Analysts estimate that a bond has an equal probability of being priced at either $940 or $1,050 one year from today. The bond is also callable at any time at $1,020. What is the expected value of this bond in one year?

Identify the general rights that are commonly granted to common stock shareholders. Answer: Common stock shareholders generally have the right to: ∙ share proportionally in dividends. ∙ share proportionally in assets remaining after liabilities have been paid in a liquidation. ∙ vote on stockholder matters of great importance, such as a merger or acquisition. ∙ vote to elect directors to the board. ∙ share proportionately in any new stock offering. Difficulty: 1 Easy Section: 15.1 Some Features of Common and Preferred Stocks Topic: Shareholder voting Bloom's: Understand AACSB: Reflective Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Explain some of the means by which a select group of shareholders can retain control over a corporation while still raising equity capital outside of their group. Answer: Some of the means include straight voting for the firm's directors, staggered terms for the directors, and various classes of stock with unequal voting rights. Difficulty: 1 Easy Section: 15.1 Some Features of Common and Preferred Stocks Topic: Shareholder voting Bloom's: Understand AACSB: Reflective Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Explain the main differences between debt and equity. Answer: ∙ Debt generally does not provide any voting rights nor company ownership. Equity does. ∙ Interest expense is generally at least partially tax deductible, dividends paid are not. ∙ Unpaid debt is a liability of the firm and can force the firm to liquidate or reorganize. Unpaid dividends are not a liability of the firm unless declared. ∙ Interest and dividends are often treated differently for tax purposes. ∙ Dividends paid to corporate shareholders are at least partially tax exempt, interest is not. Difficulty: 1 Easy Section: 15.2 Corporate Long-Term Debt Topic: Capital structure Bloom's: Understand AACSB: Reflective Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation 21 Copyright © 2019 McGraw-Hill Education. Identify three key duties of a bond trustee. Answer: ∙ Ensure the bond indenture provisions are obeyed ∙ Manage the sinking fund ∙ Represent the bondholders if the company defaults on its payments Difficulty: 1 Easy Section: 15.2 Corporate Long-Term Debt Topic: Indenture provisions Bloom's: Understand AACSB: Reflective Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Bond issuers will call bonds when it is favorable for them to do so. The benefit the issuer receives is a cost to the bondholders. Explain some of the ways in which bondholders are protected from calls. Answer: The call provision may have a deferral period which prevents the issuer from calling the bonds within the first few years following issuance. The provision may also require the issuer to pay a call premium to help offset the loss to the bondholder. Some provisions are structured as "make-whole" calls which require the issuer to pay the approximate current value of the called bond to the bondholder.
